title: Beyond-Third-Order Quantum Coherence in Two-Dimensional Spectroscopy via Order-Selective Isolation
authors: Xue Zhang, De-Ran Zhang, Hui Dong
abstract: A central challenge in nonlinear spectroscopy is the order-selective readout of weak higher-order responses that spectrally overlap with dominant lower-order signals. This bottleneck is particularly severe in two-dimensional (2D) spectroscopy, where extending conventional phase-cycling schemes to higher orders rapidly increases measurement and analysis complexity. Here we introduce a computation-assisted strategy that combines rotating-frame acquisition with a frame-shift tracking algorithm to separate signals by their frame-dependent spectral shifts. In a rubidium vapor experiment, we use this approach to isolate a 7th-order nonlinear contribution from coexisting 3rd-order components, enabling direct access to higher-order quantum-coherence dynamics without sacrificing operation at comparatively high pulse intensities. The method is broadly compatible with multidimensional spectroscopy platforms and provides a practical route to probing many-body and collective ultrafast dynamics beyond third order.
summary: The paper introduces a novel computational technique combining rotating-frame acquisition and frame-shift tracking to overcome signal overlap issues in 2D spectroscopy. By analyzing distinct frequency shifts associated with different nonlinear orders, the authors successfully isolated a seventh-order quantum coherence signal from background third-order signals in rubidium vapor. This method offers a practical route for studying complex many-body dynamics beyond current experimental limits.
main_problem: The central challenge is achieving order-selective readout of weak, higher-order nonlinear quantum responses that spectrally overlap with strong lower-order signals in two-dimensional spectroscopy.
main_result: The authors successfully isolated a seventh-order nonlinear coherence contribution from coexisting third-order components using this novel technique. This demonstrates direct access to high-order quantum dynamics beyond the conventional third order.
method: They employ a computation-assisted strategy combining rotating-frame acquisition with a frame-shift tracking algorithm to separate signals based on their distinct, predictable spectral shifts in different measurement frames.
model_or_system: The experiments were conducted using a rubidium vapor system. The technique is designed for general applicability across multidimensional spectroscopy platforms probing many-body and collective ultrafast dynamics.
key_observables: Signal decomposition into $(2n+1)^{th}$-order components, $E^{(2n+1)}_s$, and the measurement of frame-dependent spectral shifts ($\Delta\omega_{eff}^T / \Delta\omega_{RF}$) to determine coherence order.
important_parameters: The method is effective at high pulse intensities. The analysis relies on specific fully symmetric excitation pathways ($k_s = k_2 \pm n k_1 \mp n k'_1$).
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that higher-order contributions can be uniquely identified and separated by their distinct, measurable spectral shift slopes when analyzed in rotating frames.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the frame-shift tracking separation scheme (Fig. 1) and detail the pulse sequences and signal processing procedure used to separate different order signals based on phase matching conditions (Fig. 2).
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the spectral overlap problem in high-order spectroscopy, then introduces the combined rotating-frame/frame-shift tracking method, details its application using rubidium vapor, and concludes by demonstrating the successful isolation of the 7th-order signal.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, practical methodology for probing complex, non-linear quantum dynamics in atomic systems that are otherwise obscured by spectral overlap, pushing the boundaries of ultrafast spectroscopy.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Scheme of the Frame-shift tracking separation algorithm for two-dimensional spectroscopy and the sorted 7-th signals. (a) The scheme for the separation. Three spectra are sampled under different rotation frame frequency 𝜔RF, resembling the reduction of relative speed in a moving frame with constant velocity in classical mechanics. Movement of the spectral features (blue and orange points) is created with different slopes 𝑘𝛼= −1 and 𝑘𝛽= 3 for the frequency shift 𝜔eff 𝑇of the 3rd- and 7th-order signals, respectively. (b) The experimentally sorted 7th-order signals for rubidium vapor. The signal is sorted out by tracking the movement of the orange peak in subfigure (a) with the...

Fig. 2. Pulse sequences and the procedure for extracting higher-order signals. (a) Pulse sequences of 2D spectroscopy under pump–probe geometry. The wave vectors of the two pump pulses are denoted as k1 and k1′, with k1 = k1′. The wave vector of the probe pulse is k2. Additionally, the probe pulse also acts as the local oscillator (LO), allowing for heterodyned detection of signals with wave vector k𝑠= k2. Time zero is defined as the moment when the signals are generated. (b) Signals 𝑆(𝜏,𝑇, 𝜔𝑡; 𝜙, 𝜙′, 𝜔RF) are measured in three rotating frames with the same phase cycles. In each frame, the signals are processed using phasing cycling and causality enforcement to obtain the separated spectral...

Fig. 3. Energy level diagram and the 2D spectra of 87Rb. (a) The energy levels of an individual 87Rb atom. All numerical labels in the diagram are in units of THz. (b) and (c) show the separated 2D spectra, 𝑆sep + (𝜏, 𝜔eff 𝑇, 𝜔𝑡; 𝜔RF) and 𝑆sep −(𝜏, 𝜔eff 𝑇, 𝜔𝑡; 𝜔RF), respectively under the rotating frames at 787nm.

Fig. 4. Results of the frame-shift tracking algorithm and the separated higher-order spectra. (a) Red circles show the center peak centers of the signals, extracted via two-dimensional Gaussian fitting from the 𝑆sep + (𝜏, 𝜔eff 𝑇, 𝜔𝑡; 𝜔RF) spectrum at 787nm in the rotating frame. The numbers label the peak indices. (b) Signal peaks in the rotating frame at 787nm (red points) and 790nm (blue points). The arrows indicate the peak movements. (c) Shift rates Δ𝜔eff 𝑇/Δ𝜔RF for each peak. Green pentagrams represent the signal peaks from (b), with the horizontal axis indices corresponding to those in (a). The extracted shift rates for peaks 1-6 are 3.000, -1.006, -0.968, -0.970, -0.990, and -1.004,...

Fig. 5. The 𝑆sep −(𝜏, 𝜔eff 𝑇, 𝜔𝑡; 𝜔RF) spectrum and the separated higher-order contributions. (a) The 𝑆sep −(𝜏, 𝜔eff 𝑇, 𝜔𝑡; 𝜔RF) spectrum at 787nm in the rotating frame, identical to Figs. 3(c). (b) The retrieved 0Q spectrum of the 3rd-order signal, obtained using the same retrieval procedure as described in Fig. 4. (c) The recovered 4Q spectrum of the 7th-order signal.
